Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually wait until I had a chunk of free time, then try to power through stuff all at once. Being a kinesthetic learner, I found it hard to just sit and read for hours, so I'd break things up with walks or working out. I tried to keep up with the 20 courses I was taking, but it meant I was always juggling something. Most of my studying happened in bursts rather than any organized schedule.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. Some weeks I'd barely touch certain courses, then cram before deadlines hit. I tried to have regular times, but with extracurriculars and limited resources at home, it just didn't work out that way. Deadlines basically dictated my entire life. If nothing was due soon, I'd drift toward other things that felt more pressing or interesting.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or seeing a notification that made me log in. Sometimes my peers in the study group would message me asking if I'd seen something posted, and that would prompt me to check. I wasn't one of those people who logged in just to browse around. It was always purpose-driven, like I needed to submit something or check a grade. Occasionally I'd log in just because I felt guilty about not being active.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me. I'd go through phases where I logged in more consistently, especially when peer support activities were happening and people were counting on me. But it always felt like a chore rather than something automatic. The only routine I developed was checking it Sunday nights to see what was coming up for the week.
Q5: The video content was actually pretty useful when I engaged with it properly. I watched a lot of hours because I could pause, rewind, and actually process things at my own pace. Discussion posts were helpful too because hearing how other students understood concepts gave me different perspectives. Anything hands-on or interactive felt more valuable to me since that matches how I learn best. The peer interaction stuff through the support program was surprisingly helpful for keeping me accountable.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. With limited resources and spotty internet sometimes, downloading heavy files wasn't always practical. I'd skim through things rather than deeply engaging with them. Optional quizzes and practice exercises got pushed aside constantly because they didn't count for anything. If it wasn't graded, it usually didn't make my priority list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. If it was going to help me pass or improve my grade, I was in. Stuff that felt practical or applicable to real situations also grabbed my attention more. I also valued anything where I could move around or do something active rather than just passive reading. When peers were involved and I didn't want to let them down, that added motivation too.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely different. Required stuff had this weight to it, like I had no choice but to engage even when I wasn't feeling it. Optional things felt like luxuries I couldn't afford time-wise. With 20 courses and medium stress levels, I had to prioritize ruthlessly. Optional activities always lost that battle. It's not that I didn't see their value, I just couldn't justify the time investment.
Q9: Absolutely. A lot of my actual learning happened offline. I'd download videos and watch them on the bus or during breaks at my extracurricular activities. I made physical flashcards and notes that I could carry around and study while doing other things. Sometimes I'd print materials at the library since my home setup isn't great. I also studied with friends in person, going over concepts verbally, which helped me way more than staring at a screen.
Q10: There were tons of situations like that. I'd download a lecture once, then watch it multiple times offline while exercising or commuting. I studied with my peer support group in person a lot, working through problems together on whiteboards. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I also did a lot of mental rehearsal and thinking through concepts while doing physical activities. The LMS only saw me grab a file once, not the hours I spent with it afterward.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a PDF or video once to download it, then that material would live on my phone or laptop for weeks. I'd revisit it multiple times, especially before exams. The LMS would show one access, but I might have reviewed that content five or six times offline. This was especially true for anything I could study on the go since I'm not always near good internet.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. The stress hit medium levels and I just felt overwhelmed by the sheer volume of courses. Sometimes I'd log in, see all the tasks, and just feel paralyzed. The peer support intervention actually helped during those times because having people check in on me made a difference. But there were weeks where I was just going through the motions.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from extracurriculars or stressed about money and resources, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Fatigue made me just skim through things without really absorbing them. Competing responsibilities meant I'd rush through LMS activities just to check boxes. Sometimes I'd log in late at night when I was already half-asleep, which probably wasn't productive at all. The workload across 20 courses made it impossible to give everything full attention.
Q14: Yeah, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something. There were times I'd open materials and stare at them while thinking about completely unrelated stuff. The engagement was surface-level at best during those moments. My body was present but my brain had checked out.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially anything that felt overwhelming or confusing. If an activity seemed like it would take a lot of mental energy, I'd push it off until the last possible moment. Sometimes I avoided discussion posts because I didn't feel like I had anything smart to contribute. I knew these things mattered, but the immediate pressure of other deadlines always won. It's a bad habit I'm aware of but haven't fixed.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. My LMS activity would spike dramatically right before due dates. I'd cram all my engagement into short windows rather than spreading it out. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift away from the course entirely. The system probably shows these huge spikes in my activity followed by quiet periods. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I survived managing so many courses.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback motivated me to engage more with that particular course or topic. Critical feedback sometimes made me want to avoid that area for a while, which I know isn't the right response. When feedback came quickly, I was more likely to adjust my approach. Delayed feedback felt less useful because I'd already moved on mentally. The peer support feedback was actually more impactful than instructor feedback sometimes.
Q18: The course structure mattered a lot. When things were clearly organized with obvious deadlines and expectations, I could manage better. Courses that were vague or had scattered materials made me disengage faster. Having regular checkpoints helped keep me on track even when my motivation dipped. The peer support component added structure that I wouldn't have created myself. Flexibility was good but too much flexibility meant I'd procrastinate indefinitely.
Q19: Clear deadlines and organized modules made engagement easier. Having video content I could download and watch offline was huge for my situation. The peer interaction requirements actually helped because they created accountability. What made it harder was when there was too much reading-heavy content since that doesn't match my learning style. Also, when materials weren't mobile-friendly, that created barriers given my resource limitations.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to understand but struggling. They might watch videos multiple times, search through materials repeatedly, but still not produce quality work. Or maybe they're dealing with anxiety or confusion and keep checking things without making progress. It could also be someone who's present but not really processing anything, just going through motions. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. My LMS engagement was rated low, but I still got a Merit grade. A lot of my real learning happened offline or in person with peers. I'd grab materials once and study them extensively outside the system. Someone might look disengaged online but be putting in serious work that just isn't tracked. The LMS only sees a fraction of what's actually happening with a student's learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, what you click on, and submission times. They're probably good at identifying people who are completely absent or struggling with deadlines. But they miss so much of the real picture. They can't see offline studying, peer discussions, or mental engagement. They can't tell if someone watched a video attentively or had it playing in the background. The quality of engagement is invisible to logs.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when your brain is actively working on concepts, making connections, asking questions. For me, engagement often looks like discussing ideas with peers or applying concepts to real situations. It's not about how many times I logged in or how long I spent on the platform. True engagement is internal and personal, and honestly, it's hard to measure from the outside.
Q24: The LMS would completely miss my offline study habits and the physical ways I learn best. It wouldn't show the peer support conversations that happened in person or over text. My resilience in bouncing back from tough weeks wouldn't be visible. The surprise I felt when certain approaches actually worked wouldn't register anywhere. Most importantly, it wouldn't capture that despite looking disengaged on paper, I was actually motivated and working hard in ways the system just couldn't see.
